message:
perf(search): split sqlite FTS5 fallback into rank + hydrate phases

\`search_sqlite_fts5\` ran a single SELECT that both scored by
bm25 AND pulled every hydrated column (title, content,
normalized_source_sql, origin_host, workspace, idx, created_at,
conversation id, source kind) for EVERY row that matched the
FTS predicate, then applied ORDER BY + LIMIT/OFFSET afterward.
On large corpora that means sqlite decompresses, casts, and wires
up joins for thousands of rows only to throw nearly all of them
away the moment LIMIT kicks in — the exact workload characteristic
that hybrid search sees every day.

Decompose into a two-phase query and materialize only what the
caller will actually return.

Phase 1 — \`sqlite_fts5_rank_query\` (new helper):

- SELECTs \`fts_messages.rowid\` and \`bm25(fts_messages)\` only.
  The planner can therefore satisfy the query without ever
  touching the wide-row content columns.
- Keeps the existing agent / workspace / time-range / source_id
  filter pushdown but returns \`(sql, params)\` for the caller to
  execute, so the phase is fully testable in isolation.
- ORDER BY is strengthened to \`bm25, message_key, fts_messages.rowid\`.
  The added tiebreak is deterministic across runs and across
  message-id-vs-rowid schema variants, so the phase 2 IN () set
  never depends on sqlite's internal row enumeration order.
- Uses a \`message_key_expr\` computed from the passed-in
  \`uses_message_id\` so the caller owns the schema decision and
  the same SQL shape is reused by phase 2.

Phase 2 — \`sqlite_fts5_hydrate_query\` (new helper):

- Given the rowids that survived LIMIT/OFFSET in phase 1, hydrate
  the full column set (title, content, agent, workspace,
  source_path, created_at, idx, conversation id, normalized
  source_id, origin_host, source kind) with a
  \`WHERE fts_messages.rowid IN (placeholders)\` predicate.
- Reuses the same \`message_key_expr\` from phase 1, so the join
  against \`messages\` / \`conversations\` / \`sources\` stays
  consistent across the two phases.
- Emits a \`SELECT fts_messages.rowid, ...\` prefix so the caller
  can correlate each hydrated row back to the phase-1 score and
  preserve the bm25-determined ordering.

Driver \`search_sqlite_fts5\`:

- Now orchestrates phase 1 → phase 2: run the rank SQL, collect
  the surviving (rowid, bm25) pairs in rank order, early-return
  on empty, build a row-count-bounded hydrate SQL, run it, and
  stitch the hydrated content back to phase-1 order. Unchanged:
  the FTS-presence short-circuit, the schema probe via
  \`sqlite_fts_uses_message_id_column\`, the
  \`transpile_to_fts5\` pre-normalization, and the field-mask
  handling for title/content/snippet.

Dead-code removal:

- Delete the module-private \`franken_query_rows_retry\` helper.
  It was only used by the old single-query form of
  search_sqlite_fts5; both new phases use the existing
  \`franken_query_map_collect_retry\` so the retry/backoff logic is
  single-sourced.

Type aliases:

- Add \`SqliteFtsHydratedRow\` (12-tuple, matches the hydrate
  query's SELECT order) so the driver's typed decode loop stays
  readable.

Regression test added:

- \`sqlite_fts5_ranked_phase_defers_content_decode_until_after_limit\`
  builds the phase-1 SQL directly via
  \`SearchClient::sqlite_fts5_rank_query\` and asserts it does NOT
  reference \`fts_messages.title\` or \`fts_messages.content\` —
  the structural property that makes the decomposition pay off.
  Any future refactor that collapses the phases back together will
  trip this test.

Behavior for callers is unchanged. Row ordering matches the
previous ORDER BY behavior for the cases that previously had
deterministic ordering, and is strictly more deterministic for
ties.
